paper_title: REVES: REvision and VErification--Augmented Training for Test-Time Scaling

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Test-time scaling via sequential revision has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. However, standard post-training methods primarily optimize single-shot objectives, creating a fundamental misalignment with multi-step inference dynamics. While recent work treats this as multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL), conventional approaches optimize over the multi-step trajectories directly, failing to further exploit the high-quality mistakes in intermediate steps that model can learn from correcting them. We propose a two-stage iterative framework that alternates between online data/prompt augmentation and policy optimization. By converting the intermediate steps (``near-miss'' answers) in the successful recovery trajectories into decoupled revision and verification prompts, our approach concentrates training on both effective answer transformation and error identification. This approach enables efficient off-policy data generation and reduces the computational overhead of long-horizon sampling compared to standard multi-turn RL. On LiveCodeBench, using publicly available test cases as feedback, we observe gains of +6.5 points over the RL baseline and +4.0 points over standard multi-turn training. Beyond coding, our approach matches the previously reported SOTA result on circle packing while using the smallest base model (4B) and far fewer rollouts than the much larger evolutionary search systems. Math results under ground-truth verification further confirm improved correction ability. It also generalizes to out-of-distribution constraint-satisfaction puzzles such as n\_queens and mini\_sudoku, where correctness is defined entirely by problem constraints. Code is available at https://github.com/yxliu02/REVES.git.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) deployed in challenging settings rarely produce correct answer on the first attempt; real-world workflows routinely loop back with feedback. growing family of test-time scaling (TTS) algorithms formalizes this pattern, including sequential revision (Madaan et al., 2023; Shinn et al., 2023), tree search (Inoue et al., 2025), evolutionary refinement (Lee et al., 2025b), and best-of-N with verifier (Chow et al., 2025). They all share single primitive: given problem, previous attempt, and feedback, produce an improved response. This raises basic question: can we design post-training algorithm that explicitly improves models test-time sequential revision capability? We focus on sequential revision (SR) because gains on SR transfer to the rest of the family: every revision-using TTS algorithm eventually calls the policy on revise-from-prior-attempt inputs. SR is also strong TTS algorithm in its own right, matching or surpassing tree search and evolutionary refinement on planning tasks (Figure 2). Theorem 3.1 shows that increasing the policys one-step revision value on SR-induced inputs also raises Jϕ for every revision-using TTS algorithm that covers SRs revision inputs, up to small drift terms. Work done during an internship at Amazon. 1 How, then, do we post-train an LLM to optimize the SR objective JϕSR? Standard posttraining, RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022), RLVR, and GRPO (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025), optimizes single-shot expected reward, which is fundamentally misaligned with the multi-step nature of test-time deployment. The natural fix is multi-turn RL on revision rollouts, but trajectory-level credit broadcasts assign path-dependent credit. Take wrong, wrong, correct rollout: every per-turn log-probability gradient receives the same positive credit, including the wrong intermediates. Per rollout this credit is biased, and only cancels in expectation across many rollouts. We propose REVES, two-stage iterative framework that targets the SR objective JϕSR directly through its per-state structure. We show that JϕSR decomposes exactly into weighted sum of per-state one-step recovery probabilities along visited rollouts (Lemma 4.1), exposing single-state, single-turn gradients with no horizon credit assignment. REVES is an offline realization of this per-state training signal: each epoch, it runs SR rollouts under the current policy, retains those that succeed within budget, converts the intermediate (near-miss) answers into decoupled revision and verification prompts, and trains with standard singleturn RL on the augmented prompt set; the next epoch refreshes the augmentation. This concentrates training on per-state recovery and avoids both the bias of trajectory broadcasts and the cost of long-horizon online sampling. Empirically, on LiveCodeBench with public test cases as feedback, REVES achieves +6.5 points over the single-shot RL baseline and +4.0 points over standard multi-turn training. On the circle packing benchmark, Qwen3-4B base trained with REVES matches the previously reported best results from much larger evolutionary search systems built on Gemini-2.0 Pro/Flash and Qwen3-8B. Math benchmarks (MATH500, AIME24/25) show consistent improvements under both ground-truth and self-confidence stopping, and the trained policies generalize to out-of-distribution constraint-satisfaction puzzles (n_queens, mini_sudoku). Beyond standalone SR, REVES improves every revision-using TTS algorithm we tested (MCTS, AB-MCTS variants, Mind Evolution), in line with the conditional bound in Theorem 3.1. Our contributions are threefold: 1. Conceptual. We formulate test-time scaling as meta-RL problem and show that improving sequential revision also improves any revision-using TTS algorithm that covers SRs revision inputs (Theorem 3.1). 2. Methodological. We exactly decompose JϕSR into per-state one-step recovery probabilities (Lemma 4.1) and propose REVES, two-stage framework that recasts intermediate nearmiss answers as decoupled revision and verification prompts trained with single-turn RL, avoiding the path-dependent credit assignment of multi-turn RL. 3. Empirical. REVES delivers consistent test-time gains across math, coding, out-ofdistribution puzzles, and circle packing, and improves every revision-using TTS algorithm we evaluated.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Test-time-aware post-training. growing line of work shows the importance of aligning the post-training objective with the intended test-time inference strategy. For parallelsampling families, this includes optimizing pass@k (Chen et al., 2025; Walder & Karkhanis, 2025; Tang et al., 2025) when deployment uses best-of-N, majority voting (Wang et al., 2023), or learned verifiers (Chow et al., 2025). Our deployment is structurally different: the test-time strategies we care about, including sequential revision, MCTS variants, and Mind Evolution, are all revision-using algorithms whose calls take as input previous attempt together with its feedback. Aligning training with this inference family therefore requires different objective. Training sequential revision capability. Closer to our setting, several prior methods explicitly train the policy to revise, including supervised and preference-based approaches (Qu et al., 2024; Xiong et al., 2025), RL-based approaches (Kumar et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2025; 2 Lee et al., 2025a), and multi-turn RL methods that incorporate per-turn natural-language or numerical critiques (Jain et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025b;a; Zhang et al., 2026). Our work shares the broader goal of strengthening the models revision capability, but differs in framing: we primarily focus on how to align training with the test-time objective of revision-using inference strategy. Weakness-driven data synthesis and guided exploration. SwS (Liang et al., 2025) synthesizes new problems targeting the models weaknesses using strong external teacher models, and POPE (Qu et al., 2026) uses privileged oracle hints to guide on-policy exploration on hard problems. These methods are orthogonal to ours: they target single-shot pass@1, whereas REVES targets fundamentally different test-time objective.

sections.title: 3 Test-Time Scaling as a Meta-RL Problem
sections.content: Setup and two motivating examples. We fix notation used throughout the paper. Denote by the distribution over input problems, the answer space, and : [0, 1] the ground-truth reward, where r(x, y) = 1 indicating fully correct answer. We focus on the verifiable-reward regime where is computable during both training and at test time, e.g., public test-case execution for code, and constraint satisfaction for puzzles. When is unavailable at test time, surrogate stopping rule replaces it (Section 4). We model the LLM as policy πθ in RL parameterized by θ. At deployment, an LLM rarely operates by single call to πθ; instead test-time scaling (TTS) algorithm ϕ orchestrates up to policy calls and extracts final answer ˆy Y. Two canonical examples bracket the design space: Best-of-N (BoN) (Chow et al., 2025) draws candidates in parallel from πθ( x) and selects ˆy via an external scorer; every call uses the same original prompt. Sequential Revision (SR) (Madaan et al., 2023) generates an initial response y1 πθ( x) from prompt x, and for = 2, . . . , produces revision yt πθ( x, yt1, ft1) conditioned on the previous attempt yt1 and feedback signal ft1 (e.g., compiler error messages in code generation), halting at the first correct response. Detailed pseudocode is in Protocol 1 of Appendix A. General formulation: the TTS-induced decision process. Pairing any TTS algorithm ϕ with πθ induces decision process with prompt-state space and response space Y. At each step t, the policy emits response yt πθ( st), and ϕ constructs the next prompt via transition kernel Pϕ(st+1 s1:t, y1:t). The initial state is s1 = x. Note that the policy itself remains Markov in the current state (at every step, πθ conditions only on the local prompt st); the history dependence is in the kernel, because ϕ may branch from any earlier response. We focus on the family ΦR of TTS algorithms that issue at least one revision call on prior attempt; this includes Sequential Revision and adaptive tree search such as AB-MCTS (Inoue et al., 2025). For such algorithms revision-step prompt has the form st+1 = (x, yit , fit ), it {1, . . . , t}, where the prior step it is chosen by ϕ: for SR, it = (always the most recent response), so st+1 depends only on (st, yt) and the induced process is standard MDP; for tree-search algorithms such as AB-MCTS, it may be any earlier response that ϕs search policy decides to branch from, and the kernel is genuinely history-dependent. The procedure runs until random stopping time τ := min{t : r(x, yt) = 1} and outputs ˆy = yτ; that is, the procedure either early-stops once verified-correct response is produced, or exhausts the budget K. Running πθ inside ϕ yields the TTS-induced objective Jϕ(θ) = xX , (s1:τ,y1:τ )(πθ,ϕ)[r(x, yτ)], to be contrasted with the standard single-shot objective JOneShot(θ) = xX , yπθ (x)[r(x, y)], which is what RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022), RLVR, and GRPO (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025) optimize. Optimizing Jϕ rather than JOneShot is meta-RL problem (Figure 1): the policy must adapt across the distribution of multi-step contexts that ϕ generates, not just answer the original question. Our goal is to improve Jϕ for every TTS algorithm ϕ ΦR. This raises an important question: does there exist training target Jϕtrain whose optimization provably improves Jϕ for every ϕ ΦR? If so, we can train against one algorithm and have 3 (a) Standard RL: single call (b) Meta-RL inside ϕ: calls πθ r(x, y) s1 = πθ one prompt, one response, one reward πθ y2 s2 ϕ r(x, ˆy) ˆy ϕ ϕ uses the full history (s1:t, y1:t); same πθ at every call. Figure 1: (a) Standard RL: policy πθ maps problem to response with reward r(x, y). (b) TTS algorithm ϕ invokes the same πθ over up to steps, each prompt st built from the prior history (dashed arrows), and extracts final response ˆy = yτ at stopping time τ K; πθ must therefore behave well across the whole context distribution induced by ϕ. the gains transfer to every other deployment-time choice in ΦR, side-stepping the cost of running tree search or external verifiers during training. The answer is affirmative. The key fact is that every algorithm in ΦR at some point invokes πθ to revise previous attempt, so raising the revision value on training targets inputs improves every member of ΦR that covers those inputs. It does not: two policies can be indistinguishable under JOneShot on every problem in yet differ substantially under JϕSR. Theorem 3.2 (Objective mismatch). For any revision length 2, there exist policies π1, π2, (K) > 0, and problem distribution such that JOneShot(π1) = JOneShot(π2), while JϕSR (π2) JϕSR (π1) (K). The implication is direct: JOneShot and JϕSR are different objectives, and policies with identical single-shot performance can have substantially different sequential-revision performance. 4 Figure 2: DeepSeek-V3 on TravelPlanner. Left: sequential revision vs. Mind Evolution / MCTS / AB-MCTS. Right: cost-accuracy as the revision context window covers the most recent responses, {1, 2, 3}, or the full history. Our problem reduces to single concrete question: how do we post-train πθ so that JϕSR goes up? The next section presents our algorithm. Takeaway: we adopt the sequential-revision objective JϕSR as our training target. Improving test-time performance is not the same as optimizing single-shot pass@1 (Theorem 3.2). Among test-time scaling algorithms, sequential revision is both empirically strong and the one whose gains transfer to every other revision-using algorithm (Theorem 3.1), which makes it the right objective to optimize.

Since JϕSR is nonnegativeand optimize the per-state factor Vπ(z) = weighted sum of these factors, raising Vπ at any visited directly raises JϕSR , without horizon credit assignment. Comparison with multi-turn RL. Multi-turn RL provides an unbiased policy-gradient estimator for the trajectory-level objective. However, its per-turn learning signal is highly indirect. Consider wrongwrongcorrect rollout: the gradients for all three turns are weighted by the same positive terminal advantage, including the two turns that produced incorrect intermediate responses. The branches at each visited are fresh samples from πθ( z); their verifier outcomes provide yπθ (z)[r(x, y)]. the per-state estimate of Vπ(z) = trajectory, but it yields high-variance and coarse signal for improving the local revision behavior at each intermediate state. Our method instead reuses such incorrect intermediate responses as revision states. Thus, the intermediate wrong step recovery probability Vπ(z) = Pryπ(z) response is not treated as an action to imitate; it is treated as state from which the model should learn to recover. This converts successful long-horizon trajectory into several short-horizon revision problems, providing sharper training signal for local recovery while relying on the current policy to discover useful intermediate states. From recovery target to algorithm. An ideal on-policy implementation of Lemma 4.1 draws ρθ by running SR and runs single-turn RL at each z. Three design choices turn this into the algorithm we use. (1) Verification prompts alongside revision data. At test time the verifier is typically unavailable. We therefore include both revision prompts and verification prompts (asking the policy to judge whether an intermediate response is correct) in the augmented data, so the trained policy can self-stop at deployment. The split of gains across the two prompt types is in Table 5. (2) Offline generation. An on-policy estimator of JϕSR would need fresh SR rollouts every gradient step, which is intrinsically serial and prohibitively slow. We instead generate visited states once per epoch in Stage I, reuse them through Stage II, and refresh at the next epoch. (3) Successful-trajectory filtering. For far-from-optimal base policy, online samples at states from failed rollouts rarely produce verifier-accepted recovery and waste tokens. We restrict Stage to states on trajectories that succeed within budget K, where at least one recovery target exists by construction; the filter loosens as πθ improves. Two-stage realization. Stage (Data augmentation). For each input x, run SR under the current πθ: y1 πθ( x) and yt πθ( x, yt1, Feedback(yt1)) for = 2, . . . , K, terminating at the first correct response or after attempts. Discard trajectories that fail or are already correct at y1. From each retained trajectory (x, y1, . . . , yℓ) with r(x, yℓ) = 1, build (i) revision data {Revision Prompt(x, yi)}ℓ i=1, where each prompt asks the model to revise the previous response, and (ii) verification data {Verification Prompt(x, yi)}ℓ i=1, where each prompt asks the model to judge whether yi is correct. Templates are in Appendix A. Stage II (Single-turn RL). Train πθ on the union of the original RL prompts and the prompts Figure 4: Overview of the proposed RL-based data augmentation framework. In Stage I, the current policy performs sequential revision; only trajectories that succeed within budget are retained, and their intermediate states yield revision and verification prompts that are merged with the original RL prompt set. In Stage II, the policy is updated by single-turn RL on the augmented set, and the new policy regenerates the augmentation in the next epoch. produced in Stage I. The updated policy serves as the rollout policy for the next epochs Stage I. Figure 4 shows the loop. Efficiency. Our algorithm is also more efficient than standard multi-turn training. First, augmented samples are generated in an off-policy manner. With well-designed infrastructure, data augmentation can run in parallel with RL training, so the model continues training on the original RL prompts while new augmented data are generated asynchronously, avoiding any interruption to the main training loop. Second, training itself is single-turn. Each gradient step processes one (prompt, response) pair as in ordinary RLVR, with no per-step sequential rollout in the inner loop, which is the dominant cost of multi-turn RL with budget K. Appendix F.2 reports the wall-clock comparison. Test-time stopping rule. At test time the oracle stopping time τ is almost always unavailable, so we adopt surrogate stopping time ˆτ that replaces with domain-specific signal. For mathematical reasoning tasks, we leverage the Tail Confidence metric of Fu et al. (2025) as the surrogate signal. At each revision step t, the token-level confidence at position of yt is Ci = 1 j=1 log Pi(j), where Pi(j) is the probability of the j-th most likely token (a larger Ci indicates more concentrated, more confident distribution). The responselevel score ct averages Ci over the tail positions Ttail of yt. The stopping time is ˆτ := min for predefined threshold c. If ˆτ = (budget exhausted without trigger), we output ˆy = yt with = arg max1tK ct, breaking ties uniformly at random when several responses share the same confidence. Selecting the most confident response across all revisions instead of just yK improves robustness under small budgets by mitigating noise in confidence estimates at the stopping step. 3 : ct/t j=1 cj > (cid:110) (cid:111) For coding tasks, we use publicly available test cases as surrogate reward : {0, 1}, with r(x, y) = 1(y passes all public test cases). The stopping time is ˆτ := min{t : r(x, yt) = 1}, and we output ˆy = yK when ˆτ = K.

sections.title: 5 Experimental Results
sections.content: Models and data. We train Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen2.5-3B (Yang et al., 2024), Qwen3-4B(nonthinking) (Yang et al., 2025) and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025) directly with RL. Training data is sampled from Skywork/Skywork-OR1-RL-Data (7298 math + 1015 coding = 8313 examples). We evaluate on MATH500 and AIME 24/25 for math; 7 LiveCodeBench (Jain et al., 2024) and CodeContest (Li et al., 2022) for coding; and n_queens / mini_sudoku from ReasoningGym (Stojanovski et al., 2025) for out-of-distribution puzzles. Baselines and implementation. We compare against RL (single-response RL, no revision), MultiTurn (multi-turn rollouts without explicit self-verification), and PAG (Jiang et al., 2025) (multi-turn with self-verification via designed reward). Full details in Appendix E. 5.1 Main Results Consistent improvements on coding tasks. Table 1 demonstrates the benefits of REVES on coding benchmarks. Under test-time surrogate stopping criteria, where publicly available test cases are used to determine when to stop and execution results are provided as feedback for subsequent revisions (see Appendix for an example), REVES consistently improves performance across different test-time budgets, demonstrating robustness beyond symbolic rewards. Additional results on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025) are provided in Appendix G. Table 3 shows that REVES substantially improves sequential revision performance across all evaluated math benchmarks. Under oracle stopping (the Oracle-32 and Oracle-4 columns, where the rollout halts as soon as generated answer matches the ground truth, isolating the models ability to revise an incorrect solution into correct one), our method achieves consistently large gains. These improvements persist under the SelfConf-4 column, the practical test-time stopping rule based on the models own confidence, indicating that the learned capability transfers beyond the oracle setting. Matching state-of-the-art on circle packing. We further evaluate REVES on the circle packing benchmark, where the goal is to pack unit-area circles into unit square to maximize the sum of their radii. We directly optimize Eq. (1) on this task. With Qwen34B, our trained policy matches the previously reported best results from much larger evolutionary search systems built on Gemini-2.0 Pro/Flash and Qwen3-8B  (Table 2)  . JϕSR-trained policies also improve other TTS algorithms. Table 4 shows that models trained with REVES consistently improve the performance of wide range of test-time algorithms that rely on sequential revision, including MCTS (Inoue et al., 2025), AB-MCTS variants (Inoue et al., 2025) and Mind Evolution (Lee et al., 2025b). Takeaway: REVES delivers consistent, transferable test-time gains. REVES improves sequential revision on code and math, matches state-of-the-art on frontier open problems (circle packing) with far smaller model, and generalizes to out-of-distribution puzzles despite training only on math and code. As predicted by Theorem 3.1, the gains further transfer to other revision-using TTS algorithms. 5.2 Ablation Study The role of revision and verification prompts. As shown in Table 5, we investigate the roles of revision and verification prompts in sequential revision training. Qwen-2.5-7BRevisionOnly augments RL-generated data using only revision prompts, while Qwen2.5-7B-VerificationOnly augments data using only verification prompts. Qwen-2.5-7BREVES combines both prompts during data augmentation. Results under Oracle-4 indicate that revision prompts primarily improve sequential revision capability, whereas verification prompts do not directly enhance revision but instead train the model to assess solution validity. To isolate the effect of verification prompts, we evaluate confidence calibration using Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) on AIME25. For each problem, we sample multiple model responses, label them as correct or incorrect based on the final answer, and compute the probability that randomly sampled correct response receives higher TailConfidence score than randomly sampled incorrect one. The final AUROC is obtained by averaging over problems with sufficient correct samples. Under this evaluation, Qwen-2.5-7B-REVES achieves higher AUROC (74.1%) than RevisionOnly (72.1%), demonstrating that verification prompts improve confidence calibration. Thus, combining revision and verification prompts yields consistent gains by jointly enhancing revision capability and confidence estimation. Necessity of continual data augmentation. As shown in Figure 6 (right), augmenting data only at the initial epoch leads to substantially worse performance than continuous augmentation. This is because once the model stops making earlier mistakes, stale augmented data no longer provides informative learning signals, whereas regenerating data each epoch keeps the supervision aligned with the models current failure modes. Effect of augmentation budget. We study the effect of the augmentation budget on training performance. As shown in Figure 6 (left), increasing the augmentation budget consistently improves performance, indicating that our method exhibits strong scaling behavior with respect to the augmentation budget. sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We show that standard single-shot RL objectives are misaligned with test-time sequential revision, and propose REVES, which pairs RL with continual, mistake-driven data augmentation so the model learns both to revise incorrect solutions and to identify errors. Augmentation must be continual, since static data becomes uninformative once mistakes disappear. Across math, coding, and out-of-distribution tasks, REVES improves test-time sequential-revision performance over the single-shot RL and multi-turn baselines under fixed budgets, and, as predicted by our transfer analysis, these gains further carry over to other revision-using test-time algorithms. On the circle packing benchmark, Qwen3-4B trained with REVES matches the previously reported state-of-the-art achieved by much larger evolutionary search systems, using the smallest base model and far fewer rollouts.

A.2 Example Coding Task At test time for coding tasks, we follow an iterative execution-based evaluation protocol. The model first generates candidate program, which is then executed against the publicly available test cases provided by the problem. If the program fails any test, the resulting error messages or failing test feedback are collected and appended to the context for the next generation step, allowing the model to revise its solution based on execution feedback. Note 17 that passing the public test cases does not guarantee correctness: solution is considered correct only if it passes all test cases, including both public and private ones. Input (Turn 1) You are an expert Python programmer. You will be given problem specification and must produce correct Python program that passes the tests. Question: You are given permutation = (P1, P2, . . . , PN) of (1, 2, . . . , N). You want to satisfy Pi = for all = 1, 2, . . . , by performing the following operation zero or more times: Choose an integer such that 1 N. sections.title: B Limitation and Future Direction
sections.content: Limitation. REVES requires ground-truth verifier during training. Math, coding, puzzle, and circle-packing benchmarks all admit deterministic verifiers, so REVES applies directly. For tasks without such verifiers (free-form QA, creative writing, RLHF-style preference learning), REVES does not directly extend; the verification prompts trained alongside revision data can be repurposed for self-verification at test time, but their reliability depends on the policys own calibration. Future direction. The verifier requirement points to natural class of problems where REVES is especially well suited: scientific tasks whose objectives admit clear, automated reward. Examples include solving partial differential equations (PDEs), where residual norms or analytic checks serve as verifiers; symbolic regression, where fit and complexity supply numeric score; theorem proving, where proof checker provides binary feedback; and discovery-style optimization tasks like circle packing (Appendix H), where the verifier is the geometric solver itself. In all of these settings, the cost of producing the verifier signal is offset by an essentially unlimited supply of training problems and precise notion of correctness, both of which line up well with REVESs reliance on per-state one-step recovery as the gradient signal. Scaling REVES to such scientific-discovery domains is natural next step.

sections.title: D Training Prompt Template
sections.content: In this section, we present the training prompt templates used for data augmentation. An important distinction from the test-time setting is that we do not append execution error information to the context during training. Instead, as illustrated in Figure 12 and Figure 13, the training prompts rely on structured supervision rather than explicit error feedback. sections.title: E Implementation Details
sections.content: In this section, we provide details of the training and evaluation implementations used in our experiments. All experiments are conducted on machines equipped with 8 NVIDIA H200 GPUs. 23 Prompt Template for Verification in Math Tasks Problem frequency distribution of the scores for Mr. Sampsons algebra class is shown. What percent of the class received score in the 60% 69% range? Test Scores 90% 100% 80% 89% 70% 79% 60% 69% Below 60% Frequencies IIII IIII IIII IIII II IIII II Proposed Answer To solve this problem, we first identify the number of students in each score range from the frequency distribution. The total number of students is computed as 4 + 8 + 6 + 5 + 2 = 25. Among them, 5 students fall into . . . Instruction: Judge whether the proposed answer is correct. Respond concisely and end the final line with VERDICT: True or VERDICT: False . Figure 9: Prompt template used for the verification stage. The verifier observes the problem and proposed solution and outputs binary correctness judgment. RL Baseline. Our RL baseline is implemented using the official Skywork-OR1 codebase. We directly adopt their provided training scripts and configurations, which optimize single-shot response using reinforcement learning. No revision or multi-turn generation is involved during training for this baseline. Multi-Turn and PAG Baselines. The Multi-Turn and PAG baselines are implemented based on the Policy-As-GenVerifier codebase. To ensure comparability with our setting, we make two modifications to the original implementation. First, we increase the maximum response length to 8092 tokens to match our training setup. Second, we adapt the reward computation function so that it correctly evaluates responses under our datasets, while keeping the reward definition itself identical to that used in our methods. Apart from these changes, the original training pipelines and optimization settings are preserved. REVES (Ours). Our method builds on top of the same training framework as the baselines. The key difference lies in the data augmentation procedure. Before training, we first perform an offline augmentation pass to initialize the augmented dataset. During training, at the beginning of each epoch, we further augment the dataset by generating new revision trajectories using the current policy, and the newly generated samples are added to the training data. When generating augmented data, we allow maximum revision budget of = 8. The training prompts used for augmentation follow the templates described in Appendix D. The model is then trained on the mixture of original and augmented data using the same RL objective as the baseline. Evaluation Protocol. Unless otherwise specified, all experiments are evaluated using our unified evaluation framework, which will be publicly released upon paper acceptance. Given an input problem, the model follows the corresponding test-time protocol (e.g., sequential revision when applicable) to generate final response. The generated output is serialized into JSON file and evaluated by an external, task-specific verifier. This pipeline is applied uniformly across all tasks and methods to ensure fair comparison: generation and evaluation are fully decoupled, and correctness is determined solely by the external verifier rather than any internal model signals. During evaluation, the maximum response length is set to 16,384 tokens for coding tasks and AIME24/25, and 8,096 tokens for MATH500. 24 The decoding temperature is fixed to 0.6 for coding tasks and 1.0 for all other tasks, and the confidence threshold in the SelfConf setting is set to = 0.5. For math tasks, when evaluating the Multi-Turn and PAG baselines, we follow the original evaluation implementation provided in the Policy-As-GenVerifier repository to remain faithful to their protocol. Under this evaluation pipeline, we use the same maximum response length settings for 7B models as described above. For 3B models with revision budget = 4, where the effective sequence length exceeds the model context limit, we reduce the maximum response length to 4,096 tokens. All math evaluations are conducted using math_eval, ensuring that results are directly comparable across evaluation settings. Method Details: Multi-Turn Comparison, Efficiency, and Test-Time

sections.title: Sensitivity
sections.content: F.1 Comparison with Naive Multi-Turn RL natural alternative to our offline scheme is online sequential revision with standard multi-turn credit assignment. Two such schemes appear in the recent literature. The first, trajectory-level broadcast, samples trajectories of turns per prompt, scores each trajectory by its final reward, computes trajectory-level advantage, and broadcasts that single advantage uniformly to every turn in the trajectory. The second, pooled per-response, pools all responses across the trajectories, treats each response as an independent sample, and computes advantages within the pool. Neither scheme suits our setting. Sequential revision is structurally different from vanilla multi-turn task: each turn is fresh attempt at the same problem x, not step in longerhorizon decision process with intermediate sub-rewards. Trajectory-level broadcast assigns identical credit to every turn and therefore ignores that some intermediate responses are much closer to correctness than others. Pooled per-response also fails to capture this structure: the wrong intermediate responses are treated identically, even though the nearmiss failures are exactly the ones we most want the policy to learn from. Our offline selectionthen-replay scheme bypasses both pathologies. We identify near-miss failures explicitly through the success-trajectory filter and replay them as single-turn revision prompts, where standard single-turn credit assignment applies cleanly. F.2 Wall-Clock Efficiency Beyond avoiding credit-assignment pitfalls, our offline scheme is substantially faster than online multi-turn training. Multi-turn RL pays length-M serial sequential-revision cost per prompt at every gradient step, multiplied by the rollouts needed to estimate the advantage. Our method pays the length-K serial cost only once per prompt at the offline stage, and during the online RL stage performs only single-turn sampling on the extracted prompts, which can be parallelized across Best-of-N samples. Single-turn parallel sampling is much cheaper per gradient step than M-turn serial generation across rollouts, since serial generation does not benefit from the parallelism that modern inference engines provide for single-turn sampling. Empirically, on 8 NVIDIA H200 GPUs, three full iterations of our method completed in roughly 30 hours, while the equivalent online multi-turn baseline required over 48 hours on identical hardware  (Table 6)  . With more carefully optimized infrastructure, Stage data generation and Stage II RL training could run concurrently (Stage II first trains on the original RL data while Stage generates augmented data asynchronously), yielding further gains. F.3 Sensitivity to the Confidence Threshold We study the sensitivity of our test-time stopping rule to the confidence threshold used in the TailConfidence stopping criterion (Section 4). Table 7 reports accuracy and the average number of revision rounds used at budget = 16 across three math benchmarks. Higher 25 Table 6: Per-iteration wall-clock breakdown of our method on 8 NVIDIA H200 GPUs. The number of rounds also grows with the threshold, reflecting the cost-quality trade-off. sections.title: G Additional Experiments and Ablations
sections.content: We additionally evaluate our method using strong reasoning-oriented base model, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025), and compare against both the original base model and an RL baseline. Due to the substantially longer responses produced by this model (approximately 9k tokens on average, compared to 2k tokens for Qwen-7B), multi-turn training becomes significantly more expensive, which limits the scale of these experiments. As shown in Table 8, our REVES model consistently improves test-case-based performance over both the base model and the RL baseline. We expect larger gains with increased data scale and longer training.

sections.title: H Circle Packing Implementation Details
sections.content: This section gives the implementation details behind the circle packing results in Table 2. Circle packing maximizes the sum of radii of non-overlapping circles inscribed in the unit square, with verifier feedback given by non-overlap and inscription check together with the realized objective value. We instantiate REVES on this benchmark by directly optimizing JϕSR in the recovery form of Lemma 4.1: each visited state zt corresponds to partially-improved candidate configuration, the policy proposes refined configuration Table 8: Performance on LiveCodeBench under different evaluation periods. Reward shaping. The raw objective of candidate is its sum of radii g(y) := i=1 ri(y) (a continuous quantity), which clusters in narrow band near the optimum (g 2.63 for the = 26 instance we evaluate) and differs across candidates only at the third or fourth decimal. Used directly, this signal is too compressed to drive learning. We therefore shape it in two steps. Let v(y) 0 denote the total constraint violation (circle overlap plus boundary excess), so that is feasible iff v(y) = 0. First, define the raw score s(y) as s(y) = g(y), g(y) v(y), 1, v(y) = 0, v(y) > 0 and g(y) 2.64, v(y) > 0 and g(y) > 2.64, i.e., minor violations are absorbed as soft penalty, while implausibly high sums coupled with infeasibility are flagged as cheating and assigned = 1. Second, we amplify by squaring the offset from 2.5: R(s) := (s 2.5)2. Since feasible raw scores live above 2.5, this magnifies small absolute differences, e.g., = 103 gain near = 2.6 produces 2 104, which is no longer dwarfed by gradient noise. The reward used for revision from parent state is the resulting improvement, U(y x) := R(s(y)) R(s(x)), so the policy is rewarded for any move that improves on the parent and penalized for any move that worsens it. Stage I: search rollouts and prompt-set construction. We run 4 sequential-revision rollouts under the current πθ with budget = 100, where each step proposes refinement of the previous candidate configuration and the verifier returns the shaped reward U(y x) defined above. From the visited intermediate states across these rollouts, we filter to retain those whose subsequent revisions produce verified improvements, and assemble the resulting revision prompts into the augmented prompt set. Stage II: RL training. We train Qwen3-4B with GRPO (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025) on the prompt set from Stage I. Each gradient step samples 8 prompts; for each prompt we draw 64 rollouts to form the group used for the GRPO advantage estimate. representative solution.
